summary: Simpson Fiscal Court filed a response on Feb 16 defending its December data center ordinance. The county argues TenKey LandCo is subject to its zoning regulations. No court ruling yet; the case remains active in Simpson Circuit Court. TenKey seeks to invalidate the ordinance requiring conditional use permits for data centers within Franklin city limits.

Franklin Planning & Zoning Commission has tabled the vote on TenKey's preliminary development plan four times (most recently in a 3-1 vote). Commissioners cite the ongoing TenKey v. Simpson County lawsuit as reason to delay. The city also discovered its Planning and Zoning Administrator was never formally hired, adding procedural complications. No approval or denial vote has been taken.

Columbia County Board of Commissioners approved the White Oak (Trammell Crow) and Pumpkin Center data center rezonings in early February 2026, rejecting a third proposal. Citizen Gregory P. Guido Jr. filed two judicial review petitions on Feb 25 challenging both approvals, alleging procedural defects, incomplete water/sewer analysis, outdated traffic study, and conflicts with the county comprehensive plan.

Henrico's Board of Zoning Appeals unanimously denied Centra Logistics' vested-rights appeal on Feb 27, 2026, ruling the company does not have vested rights to build a data center on the Darbytown Road site. Centra is weighing whether to pursue a court appeal or proceed with an alternate plan for up to seven warehouses, which received administrative approval under existing zoning.

Microsoft's cancellation of the 244-acre Caledonia data center (Project Nova) is confirmed. The decision followed a 2,000-signature petition and strong community opposition. Microsoft said it remains committed to the region and Racine County officials are working to identify an alternative site, possibly still in Caledonia. Microsoft's other WI projects (Mount Pleasant, Kenosha) are unaffected.

The PA Game Commission approved the controversial land swap in a 6-3 vote in late January 2026, deeding 55 acres of State Game Lands 234 plus a 200-foot right-of-way to Limerick Town Center LLC. In exchange, the commission received 176 acres in Edgemont Township (Delaware County) and 377.2 acres in Bern Township (Berks County). The Limerick Board of Supervisors voted to send a letter opposing the swap. No formal data center application has been submitted yet, but one is widely expected.

Google was publicly revealed on March 3, 2026 as the company behind the proposed 1.8M sq ft, 700MW Hermantown data center. Minnesota Power signed an electric service agreement with Google. The project would be powered by 300MW wind + 400MW battery storage. However, Mortenson previously withdrew permit applications in Nov 2025 and the SHDC/MCEA lawsuit challenging the environmental review remains active. Plans are on hold while Google/Mortenson rework their approach.

Ransom Township supervisors denied Scranton Materials' zoning amendment request in a 2-1 vote on Jan 21, 2026, citing lack of expert testimony. Scranton Materials filed an appeal on Feb 27 arguing the township unlawfully excludes data centers since its zoning ordinance does not provide for them. PA municipalities are required to allow every type of lawful land use somewhere within their borders. The legal challenge is now pending.

The public hearing on Amazon's $2B data center rezoning was postponed due to a paperwork error (duplicated parcel numbers). The Planning and Zoning Commission hearing is now rescheduled for March 23, 2026. Community opposition remains strong, with dozens of residents packing prior meetings to oppose the 330+ acre industrial rezoning. No decision has been made yet.

The OpenAI/Oracle Stargate data center cleared a major legal hurdle on Feb 20, 2026. Washtenaw County Judge Julia Owdziej rejected a resident's lawsuit seeking to intervene in the consent judgment that allowed the project to proceed, ruling it was filed past the 30-day deadline. The consent judgment allowing the $7B project remains intact. Construction by Walbridge is expected to begin in early 2026, pending MPSC approval. The project includes three 550,000 sq ft buildings on 250 acres.

New Brunswick City Council voted unanimously on Feb 19, 2026 to remove data centers as a permitted use from the Jersey-Sandford Redevelopment Plan, killing the Amzak Capital-backed 27,000 sq ft AI data center proposal. The site at 100 Jersey Avenue was restored to its original designation as public park space. The decision came after hundreds of residents rallied inside and outside City Hall in opposition.

Madison Common Council passed a one-year moratorium on Jan 13, 2026 on new data centers and telecom centers larger than 10,000 sq ft. Separately, QTS formally withdrew its $12B data center proposal from the Village of DeForest on Jan 28, 2026 after the village determined the project was not feasible and announced its intention to deny the annexation of 1,600 acres from the Town of Vienna.

Hood County commissioners rejected a data center moratorium twice: first on Feb 10 and again on Feb 25, both in 3-2 votes. A letter from state Sen. Paul Bettencourt warned that counties lack constitutional authority to impose development moratoriums. Instead of pursuing another vote, commissioners agreed to request a formal opinion from AG Ken Paxton on whether the county has authority to halt development. The Sailfish/Comanche Circle 5GW data center project near Tolar continues to advance.

Fulton County commissioners voted 2-1 on March 3, 2026 to approve a one-year moratorium on data center construction, effective immediately. The Area Plan Commission had previously voted 6-1 to recommend the moratorium after a packed public hearing on Feb 11. A data center committee will be formed during the pause to study economic and environmental impacts. The moratorium was prompted by a potential 500MW data center backed by developer Decennial Group.

Lyon Township board voted unanimously in late February 2026 to enact a 180-day (6-month) moratorium on data centers and all developments over 100,000 sq ft. The moratorium allows the township planner, engineer, and consultants to review current data center regulations. However, the existing Verrus/Sidewalk Infrastructure Partners 'Project Flex' data center (six buildings on 172 acres, with conditional site plan approval from Sept 2025) is likely unaffected since it predates the moratorium.
